Issues.Title: Sign Humanizer.dll
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Sign Humanizer.dll" (#54993)

Issues.Title: Forward help arguments through `dotnetup dotnet`
Issues.Body:
`dotnetup dotnet run --help` was handled as help for `dotnetup dotnet` instead of being forwarded to the managed `dotnet` command. This affected forwarded child commands that expect to receive their own `--help` option.

- **Forwarded argument parsing**
  - Added a hidden capture-remaining argument for `dotnetup dotnet`.
  - Uses `CaptureRemainingTokens` so child-command options are preserved once forwarding starts.

```csharp
internal static readonly Argument<string[]> ForwardedArguments = new("additional arguments")
{
    Arity = ArgumentArity.ZeroOrMore,
    CaptureRemainingTokens = true,
    Hidden = true,
};
```

- **Command execution**
  - Reads forwarded arguments from the capture-remaining argument instead of `ParseResult.UnmatchedTokens`.

- **Regression coverage**
  - Added parser and execution coverage for forwarding `dotnet run --help` as `["run", "--help"]`.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Forward help arguments through `dotnetup dotnet`" (#54992)

Issues.Title: Avoid parsing unused global.json in dotnetup installs
Issues.Body:
dotnetup could crash when a malformed `global.json` existed in the current directory, even for install commands that did not need to read it. The install flow now only reads `global.json` data when it can affect the request.

- **Install request resolution**
  - Added a focused global.json lookup helper for install workflows.
  - Skips global.json discovery entirely when an explicit install path and explicit channel make it irrelevant.
  - Allows path-only discovery when the file path may still be needed, without parsing contents.

- **global.json reader**
  - Extended `GetGlobalJsonInfo` with optional content parsing:
    ```csharp
    GlobalJsonModifier.GetGlobalJsonInfo(initialDirectory, parseContents: needsGlobalJsonContents);
    ```

- **Regression coverage**
  - Added tests for malformed `global.json` with explicit install path/channel.
  - Added path-only lookup coverage for malformed `global.json` when channel fallback may inspect the file separately.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Avoid parsing unused global.json in dotnetup installs" (#54991)

Issues.Title: `get-dotnetup` scripts should be on versioned urls
Issues.Body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have read the [dotnetup documentation](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/tree/release/dnup/documentation/general/dotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3Adotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup discussions](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/categories/dotnetup).

### Issue type

Bug report

### Description

Right now, the scripts point to a raw github file. We cannot do that in a stable release.
This issue tracks migrating once https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54984 and the arcade change are in https://github.com/dotnet/arcade/pull/17053 then we need to update `sdk` `main` and `release/dnup` to use the daily url artifacts. 

https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54735 also probably should have considered this, I did not know that the url for the script was not off /daily/ and not off a published artifact. 

# AI 

# Cut over dotnetup bootstrap scripts to the signed daily aka.ms link

## Summary

`scripts/get-dotnetup.ps1` is now Authenticode-signed and both `get-dotnetup.ps1` and
`get-dotnetup.sh` are published as Arcade Blob artifacts on every dotnetup CI build. Once the
supporting Arcade change has flowed into this repo and a build has published the scripts, the
new version-stable link `https://aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.ps1` will always
serve the **signed** copy. This issue tracks verifying the link is live and then repointing all
consumers/docs off the legacy unsigned link.

This is **Part 3** of a 3-part effort:

- **Part 1 — dotnet/arcade (done):** add `get-dotnetup.{ps1,sh}` to `DotnetupAkaMSCreateLinkPatterns`
  so the `dotnetup` publishing channel (id 10506) generates aka.ms links for the scripts.
- **Part 2 — dotnet/sdk (done):** sign `get-dotnetup.ps1`, stage both scripts (win-x64 leg), register
  them as Blob artifacts, and set their `RelativeBlobPath` to `dotnetup/<version>/get-dotnetup.{ps1,sh}`.
- **Part 3 — this issue:** verify the link is live, then cut over consumers and docs.

## Why

The `.ps1` is currently served raw from GitHub via a manually-created vanity link
(`aka.ms/dotnetup/get-dotnetup.ps1` → `raw.githubusercontent.com/...`). It is unsigned, so
PowerShell's default execution policy (`RemoteSigned`/`AllSigned`) can block a saved copy. Only
the `.ps1` needs signing — shell scripts have no equivalent code-signing gate on macOS/Linux —
but the `.sh` is published too so it shares the same version-stable, CI-updated link namespace as
the binaries.

## Dependency chain (must happen in order)

1. The Arcade PR (Part 1) merges and the resulting Arcade package flows into dotnet/sdk
   (`release/dnup`) via a normal dependency update. Until then, the SDK publish wiring (Part 2)
   produces the blobs but **no aka.ms link is created** for them.
2. A dotnetup CI build (definitionId 1544) runs on the updated Arcade and publishes
   `dotnetup/<version>/get-dotnetup.ps1` (+ `.sh` + `.sha512`) to the `dotnetup` channel.
3. Arcade's `LatestLinksManager` version-strips + flattens the blob path and creates/updates
   `aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.ps1` (and `.sh`).

> Note: the link prefix is fixed by the publishing infra as `aka.ms/dotnet/<channel>/<quality>/<file>`,
> so the result is `aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.ps1` — **not** the shorter
> `aka.ms/dotnetup/daily/...`.

## Verification checklist (do before cutover)

- [ ] Confirm the Arcade change merged and the Arcade dependency bumped in
      `eng/Version.Details.xml` on `release/dnup`.
- [ ] Confirm a dotnetup CI build published the script blobs (check the build's published blobs
      for `dotnetup/<version>/get-dotnetup.ps1`, `.ps1.sha512`, `.sh`, `.sh.sha512`).
- [ ] `curl -sIL https://aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.ps1` resolves to the published
      blob (not raw GitHub).
- [ ] Download the served `.ps1` and verify `Get-AuthenticodeSignature` reports `Valid` with the
      .NET signing cert (MicrosoftDotNet500).
- [ ] The served `.sha512` matches the served script bytes.

## Cutover work (this issue)

Repoint these consumers from the legacy `aka.ms/dotnetup/get-dotnetup.{ps1,sh}` to the new
`aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.{ps1,sh}`:

- [ ] `eng/dotnetup-shared.ps1` — `$getterUrl = 'https://aka.ms/dotnetup/get-dotnetup.ps1'`
      (and the comment above `Install-DotnetupFromAkaMs`).
- [ ] `eng/dotnetup-shared.sh` — `getter_url="https://aka.ms/dotnetup/get-dotnetup.sh"`
      (and the comment above `AcquireDotnetup`).
- [ ] `documentation/general/dotnetup/README.md` — the two download snippets.
- [ ] Decide the fate of the legacy vanity link `aka.ms/dotnetup/get-dotnetup.{ps1,sh}`:
      either retire it, or repoint it at the new daily link so external users with the old URL
      get the signed copy.

### Make the signature actually validate in docs

The README currently pipes straight into the interpreter:

```pwsh
iwr https://aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.ps1 | iex
```

`iwr | iex` runs the script as an in-memory expression and **bypasses execution policy entirely**,
so the Authenticode signature is never checked. For the signature to matter, document a
save-then-run pattern, e.g.:

```pwsh
$s = Join-Path $env:TEMP 'get-dotnetup.ps1'
iwr https://aka.ms/dotnet/dotnetup/daily/get-dotnetup.ps1 -OutFile $s
# Get-AuthenticodeSignature $s   # optional: inspect the signature
& $s
```

- [ ] Update README download instructions to a save-then-run pattern (keep the one-liner as a
      convenience alternative if desired).

## References

- Part 1 (Arcade): `DotnetupAkaMSCreateLinkPatterns` in
  `src/Microsoft.DotNet.Build.Tasks.Feed/src/model/PublishingConstants.cs`; channel id 10506
  (`dotnetup`). Precedent: `AspireAkaMSCreateLinkPatterns` / `get-aspire-cli.{ps1,sh}`.
- Part 2 (this repo): `src/Installer/dotnetup/dotnetup.csproj` (`StageGetDotnetupScripts`),
  `eng/Signing.props` (FileSignInfo + Blob registration), `eng/Publishing.props`
  (`SetDotnetupBlobPaths`).

### dotnetup version

_No response_
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "`get-dotnetup` scripts should be on versioned urls" (#54990)

Issues.Title: macOS x64 public CI tests consistently failing with timeouts and hangs
Issues.Body:
## Summary

The macOS x64 test leg (`osx.15.amd64.open`) in the `dotnet-sdk-public-ci` pipeline is consistently failing. These failures are not caught in PR validation because macOS x64 does not run there — they only manifest in the public CI builds against `main`.

## Failure Pattern

Out of 215 work items, 15 are failing in a recent build. The failures fall into two categories:

### 1. Timeouts (exit code -3) — 13 work items

Tests hang until the 1-hour Helix work item timeout is reached, then get killed. Example: `dotnet.Tests.dll.5` hangs indefinitely on `ItRecognizesRelativePathsForASolutionFromSubFolder`.

Affected test assemblies:
- `dotnet.Tests.dll` (shards 5, 9, 16, 22)
- `Microsoft.NET.Build.Tests.dll` (shards 2, 6, 7, 15)
- `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly.Tests.dll` (shard 1)
- `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Razor.Tests.dll` (shard 2)
- `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.StaticWebAssets.Tests.dll` (shards 14, 15)
- `dotnet-watch.Tests.dll` (shard 7)

### 2. Test host crashes (exit code 1) — 2 work items

Test host becomes unresponsive (inactivity timeout of 48 minutes triggers hang dump collection), then the process is aborted.

- `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers.UnitTests.dll` (shards 7, 22)

The crash in shard 22 occurred while running: `Microsoft.NetCore.Analyzers.Usage.UnitTests.PreferGenericOverloadsTests.MultipleTypeArgumentsWithOtherGenericArgument_OffersFixer_CS`

## Example Build

- **Build**: [dotnet-sdk-public-ci #20260625.35](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1481771)
- **Helix Job**: `91c533ba-9018-4d10-b9ba-92d6fc23a862` ([details](https://helix.dot.net/api/jobs/91c533ba-9018-4d10-b9ba-92d6fc23a862/details))
- **Queue**: `osx.15.amd64.open`
- **Branch**: `refs/heads/main`
- **Commit**: 42b15a448cc1f5274d6287ebe31a84d572e18b8a

## Impact

These failures have been happening consistently for some time. Since macOS x64 is not part of PR validation, regressions land undetected and the public CI build remains persistently red for this leg.

## Notes

- The timeouts span many different test assemblies, suggesting a systemic issue on macOS x64 rather than individual test bugs.
- All timeout failures occur on different machines (dci-mac-build-*), ruling out a single bad machine.
- The 1-hour timeout is being hit across the board, indicating tests are hanging rather than being slow.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "macOS x64 public CI tests consistently failing with timeouts and hangs" (#54988)

Issues.Title: Onboard MSBuild task projects to Microsoft.Build.TaskAuthoring.Analyzer (report-only)
Issues.Body:
## What

Onboards the repo to the **`Microsoft.Build.TaskAuthoring.Analyzer`** (the MSBuild thread-safe task analyzer) and applies it to the **12 projects that contain MSBuild task implementations**, in **report-only** mode. The repo builds **green** (0 errors, 0 warnings); every analyzer finding surfaces as an info-level *suggestion*.

This is the first step toward getting SDK's MSBuild Tasks 'green' from an analyzer perspective. Follow-up PRs can then tackle one task class — or one diagnostic category — at a time by raising severity in `.editorconfig`.

## Mechanism (centralized + opt-in)

- **`Directory.Packages.props`** — pins the analyzer (`18.9.0-preview-26310-106`, dotnet-eng feed).
- **`eng/MSBuildTaskAuthoringAnalyzer.props`** (new) — adds the analyzer as an analyzers-only `PackageReference` (`PrivateAssets="all" IncludeAssets="analyzers"`) for any project that opts in with `IsMSBuildTaskProject=true` (excluded in source-only builds). Imported from `Directory.Build.targets`.
- **12 task `.csproj` files** — each opts in via `<IsMSBuildTaskProject>true</IsMSBuildTaskProject>`.
- **`.editorconfig`** — downgrades `MSBuildTask0001`–`0005` to `suggestion`. At info severity the diagnostics are never promoted to warnings/errors — not even by the MSBuild engine's warn-as-error (`MSBuildTreatWarningsAsErrors`) that Arcade enables — so **no** `WarningsNotAsErrors` exemption lists are needed.
- **`src/StaticWebAssetsSdk/.editorconfig`** — carries its own copy of the `suggestion` block because it sets `root = true` and so does not inherit the repo-root `.editorconfig`. It's the only one of the 12 task projects with a `root = true` editorconfig.

## Diagnostics

The analyzer raises **373 unique findings** across **11** projects (`Microsoft.DotNet.GenAPI.Task` is clean):

| Rule | Count | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `MSBuildTask0002` | 49 | API needs a `TaskEnvironment` alternative (env vars, CWD, `Path.GetFullPath`, `Process.Start`) |
| `MSBuildTask0003` | 268 | File-system API needs an absolutized path |
| `MSBuildTask0005` | 56 | Transitive unsafe usage in a task call chain |

Top projects: StaticWebAssets.Tasks (144), sdk-tasks (86), Publish.Tasks (51), Build.Tasks (43).

A full report (per-rule / per-project / per-file breakdown) is attached to the tracking work.

## Re-enablement path (follow-up PRs)

- **Per category:** raise a single `dotnet_diagnostic.MSBuildTask000X.severity` to `warning`/`error` in the root `.editorconfig` (and the StaticWebAssets copy) once that rule is repo-wide clean.
- **Per task class:** add/edit an `.editorconfig` in that task's source directory raising severity, then migrate that class to `IMultiThreadableTask` / `TaskEnvironment`.

## Validation

`build.cmd` → **Build succeeded, 0 errors, 0 warnings.** No `.xlf` changes; no new NETSDK error codes.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Onboard MSBuild task projects to Microsoft.Build.TaskAuthoring.Analyzer (report-only)" (#54979)

Issues.Title: Fix flaky Nuget_reference_compat test: make TestContextOutputHelper thread-safe
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Fixes #54960

`TestContextOutputHelper` (introduced in #54769) is not thread-safe, but it is used concurrently from [`Parallel.ForEach` in `Nuget_reference_compat`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/54b99958954c7143ddd856ce7c4061a393d41469/test/Microsoft.NET.Build.Tests/GivenThatWeWantToVerifyNuGetReferenceCompat.cs#L60-L97) where multiple threads share the same `Log` instance and call `Execute()` → `TestContextOutputHelper.WriteLine()` simultaneously.

Neither `StringBuilder` nor `TestContext.WriteLine` are thread-safe, so the concurrent writes intermittently race on the internal buffer, producing:

```
System.AggregateException: One or more errors occurred.
  (Destination is too short. (Parameter 'destination'))
  ---> System.ArgumentException: Destination is too short. (Parameter 'destination')
    at System.Text.StringBuilder.AppendWithExpansion(...)
    at Microsoft.NET.TestFramework.TestContextOutputHelper.WriteLine(...)
```

## Fix

Added a `lock` around all `StringBuilder` and `TestContext` operations in `TestContextOutputHelper`. Also consolidated the duplicate `Output` property to delegate to `ToString()`.

## Impact

Test infrastructure only — no product code changes.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Fix flaky Nuget_reference_compat test: make TestContextOutputHelper thread-safe" (#54985)

Issues.Title: dotnetup should support json outputs for all commands
Issues.Body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have read the [dotnetup documentation](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/tree/release/dnup/documentation/general/dotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3Adotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup discussions](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/categories/dotnetup).

### Issue type

Feature request

### Description

`dotnetup` should support json output for all commands, so that tooling like [MAUI.Sherpa](https://github.com/Redth/MAUI.Sherpa) can more easily integrate with it. When combined with [`--dry-run`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54980) this would let tools more easily show users what will happen when they perform a command.

We should align with the SDK here, so `--format json` is probably the most compatible here (and `--format text` would be the natural 'default').

The structure of the documents emitted for each command would be unique to each command, but it would be important that if the command was run with `--dry-run` that fact is communicated in the json (`"dry-run": true`) so that consumers have enough context to understand if the action was taken or not.

### dotnetup version

_No response_
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "dotnetup should support json outputs for all commands" (#54981)

Issues.Title: dotnetup should support `--dry-run` for all commands that may modify the system
Issues.Body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have read the [dotnetup documentation](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/tree/release/dnup/documentation/general/dotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3Adotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup discussions](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/categories/dotnetup).

### Issue type

Bug report

### Description

It's common for tools of all kinds to support a 'what if'/'dry run' mode where the tool will do investigation/prep work for the command the user entered and then give a report of what _would_ happen if the user ran the command.

This can be used for things like update checks, or for building tooling on top of dotnetup.

At minimum, 
* sdk install
* sdk update
* sdk uninstall
* runtime install
* runtime update
* runtime uninstall

should support this mode.


In this mode we'd do all of the non-modifying parts of these operations:
* manifest checking
* version resolution (channel -> latest in that channel)
* comparisons to locally installed components
* lists of locally-installed components to be removed/modified
and emit those, but not actually download/remove/install the components.\

(This was requested by @Redth)

### dotnetup version

latest
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "dotnetup should support `--dry-run` for all commands that may modify the system" (#54980)

Issues.Title: migrate FilterStaticWebAssetEndpoints
Issues.Body:
Fixes dotnet/msbuild#14165

### Context

MSBuild's multithreaded execution model requires tasks to avoid process-global state (current working directory). `FilterStaticWebAssetEndpoints` absolutized asset `ContentRoot`/`RelatedAsset` paths against the process current directory, so it would resolve them incorrectly when run on a worker thread. This migrates the task to resolve paths against its per-instance `TaskEnvironment.ProjectDirectory`.

### Changes Made

- Annotated `FilterStaticWebAssetEndpoints` with `[MSBuildMultiThreadableTask]` and implemented `IMultiThreadableTask`.
- Added a `TaskEnvironment` property defaulting to `TaskEnvironment.Fallback` to preserve single-process semantics.
- Passed `TaskEnvironment` into `StaticWebAsset.ToAssetDictionary(Assets, TaskEnvironment)`, routing `ContentRoot`/`RelatedAsset` absolutization through `env.GetAbsolutePath` instead of the process current directory.
- Left the `Path.GetFullPath` canonicalization wrapper in `NormalizeContentRootPath` intact, so dictionary keys and output paths stay canonical.

### Testing

- Built `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.StaticWebAssets.Tests` — 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- Added `FilterStaticWebAssetEndpointsMultiThreadingTest`:
  - `ResolvesRelativeContentRootAgainstTaskEnvironmentProjectDirectoryNotProcessCurrentDirectory` — sets the process CWD to a decoy directory and `TaskEnvironment.ProjectDirectory` to a different directory, then asserts the resulting `AssetsWithoutMatchingEndpoints[0]` `ContentRoot` is rooted to the project directory, not the decoy CWD. The test fails if the migration is reverted.
  - Result: <N passed, M failed>.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "migrate FilterStaticWebAssetEndpoints" (#54977)

Issues.Title: Migrate remaining xUnit test projects to MSTest.Sdk on MTP
Issues.Body:
Migrates the three test projects that still used xUnit (v3) to `MSTest.Sdk` on Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP), following the pattern established by the sibling migration PRs #54883, #54862, and #54904. With these, every remaining xUnit test project covered by this effort is now on MSTest.

### Projects migrated
- `test/Microsoft.NET.Build.Containers.IntegrationTests`
- `test/TemplateEngine/.../Microsoft.TemplateEngine.Authoring.TemplateVerifier.IntegrationTests`
- `test/TemplateEngine/.../Microsoft.TemplateEngine.Authoring.TemplateVerifier.UnitTests`

### Resolving the Verify.XunitV3 blocker (why #54883 skipped the TemplateVerifier projects)
The shared `TemplateVerifier` tool's `VerificationEngine` drives snapshot verification through `Verify.XunitV3` by default. That adapter resolves the running test from xUnit's *ambient context*, which is unavailable under MSTest and fails at runtime with `TestContext.TestMethod is null`. That is precisely why #54883 left these two projects on xUnit.

To resolve it properly (rather than leave them on xUnit), this PR adds a pluggable hook to the shipping tool:
- `VerificationEngine.DirectoryVerifier` — an optional `static` override of the directory-verification entry point. When unset, the built-in xUnit verifier is used (kept behind a `[MethodImpl(NoInlining)]` `DefaultVerifyDirectory` so the `Verify.XunitV3` reference stays off the path when an override is supplied). `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` updated accordingly.
- Each migrated project installs a `ModuleInitializer` (`VerifyDirectoryInitializer`) that routes verification to `VerifyMSTest.Verifier.VerifyDirectory`, marks its snapshot test classes `[UsesVerify]`, and references `Verify.MSTest` instead of `Verify.XunitV3`.

> Note: this overlaps conceptually with #54904, which adds an equivalent hook on an older base. Current `main` already carries the newer `CustomDirectoryVerifier` content-fetcher mechanism; this change layers the `DirectoryVerifier` static hook on top of it for the default verification path.

### Infra
- `test/TemplateEngine/Directory.Build.props` now references `Microsoft.NET.TestFramework.MSTest` for `UsingMSTestSdk` projects (keeping the xUnit framework for the rest), and sets `SolutionDir`/`SolutionName` so Verify's solution auto-discovery doesn't emit a warning that `warnaserror` would promote to an error in the Helix test build.

### Containers.IntegrationTests highlights
- Custom xUnit `Docker*Fact`/`Docker*Theory` attribute subclasses → MSTest `ConditionBaseAttribute` gates (`DockerUnavailableCondition`, `PodmanCliCondition`, `ContainerdStoreUnavailableCondition`, `DockerSupportsArchCondition`), mirroring the already-migrated `Microsoft.NET.Build.Containers.UnitTests` sibling.
- `[CollectionDefinition]`/`[Collection]` (incl. `DisableParallelization`) → `[DoNotParallelize]` on the affected classes (the empty collection/fixture holders were removed; the dead `DockerTestsFixture` was deleted).
- Constructor `ITestOutputHelper` injection → `SdkTest.Log`; `[Fact]`/`[Theory]`/`[InlineData]` → `[TestMethod]`/`[DataRow]`; assertions ported to MSTest (`Assert.Throws<T>` → `Assert.ThrowsExactly<T>`, etc.).

### Parallelization
No `[assembly: Parallelize]` was added; `[DoNotParallelize]` is applied to the formerly-collection / shared-state / snapshot classes, matching the sibling-PR convention.

### Validation
All three projects build cleanly — **0 errors, 0 warnings** — against the repo-local SDK. Tests were **not** run as part of this change, matching the sibling migration PRs (which state the same).

### Open items / blockers for reviewers
- **Shipping-tool change overlaps with #54904.** Both add a `DirectoryVerifier` hook to `VerificationEngine`. They will need to be reconciled at merge; the implementations are functionally equivalent.
- **Arch-conditional data semantics.** The one method using per-row `DockerSupportsArchInlineData` (`CanPackageForAllSupportedContainerRIDs`) is already `[Ignore]`d (dotnet/sdk#49300), so its rows were converted to plain `[DataRow]`s with the arch prepended and the per-row `Skip` text dropped. If that test is ever re-enabled, the per-arch runtime gating will need to be reintroduced (e.g. via `Assert.Inconclusive`).
- Shared xUnit-coupled helper libraries (`Microsoft.NET.TestFramework`, `Microsoft.TemplateEngine.TestHelper`) were **not** modified.

Part of the xUnit → MSTest migration effort.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Migrate remaining xUnit test projects to MSTest.Sdk on MTP" (#54976)

When pull request checks pass, complete this PR by creating a merge commit, *not* a squash or rebase commit. If this repo does not allow creating merge commits from the GitHub UI, use command line instructions. ## Instructions for merging via command line Run these commands to merge this pull request from the command line. ``` sh git fetch git checkout release/10.0.4xx git pull --ff-only git checkout main git pull --ff-only git merge --no-ff release/10.0.4xx # If there are merge conflicts, resolve them and then run git merge --continue to complete the merge # Pushing the changes to the PR branch will re-trigger PR validation. git push https://github.com/dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main ```

[resolve-github]: https://help.github.com/articles/resolving-a-merge-conflict-on-github/ [resolve-cli]: https://help.github.com/articles/resolving-a-merge-conflict-using-the-command-line/ ## Instructions for updating this pull request Contributors to this repo have permission update this pull request by pushing to the branch 'merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main'. This can be done to resolve conflicts or make other changes to this pull request before it is merged. The provided examples assume that the remote is named 'origin'. If you have a different remote name, please replace 'origin' with the name of your remote. ``` git fetch git checkout -b merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main origin/main git pull https://github.com/dotnet/sdk merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main (make changes) git commit -m "Updated PR with my changes" git push https://github.com/dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main ```
or if you are using SSH ``` git fetch git checkout -b merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main origin/main git pull git@github.com:dotnet/sdk merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main (make changes) git commit -m "Updated PR with my changes" git push git@github.com:dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/release/10.0.4xx-to-main ```
Contact .NET Core Engineering (dotnet/dnceng) if you have questions or issues. Also, if this PR was generated incorrectly, help us fix it. See https://github.com/dotnet/arcade/blob/main/.github/workflows/scripts/inter-branch-merge.ps1.

Issues.Title: Add dotnetup project-local SDK setup spec
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Adds a discussion spec for project-local SDK setup in dotnetup, covering clean checkout/team bootstrap scenarios using .NET 10+ `global.json` `sdk.paths`.

This is intentionally spec-only and does not port implementation code from #54968.

## Discussion focus

- API naming alternatives such as `--local`, `--project-local`, `--repo-local`, `dotnetup sdk setup`, `dotnetup sdk bootstrap`, `--install-path .dotnet --update-global-json`, and `--install-path here`.
- Install-root tradeoffs between a repo-owned `.dotnet/` root and a dotnetup-managed hive referenced from `global.json`.
- Exact version/channel/prerelease behavior and the corresponding `global.json` `rollForward`, `allowPrerelease`, and `sdk.paths` shape.
- Merge/preservation, `.gitignore`, security, portability, update, uninstall, and GC considerations.

cc @nagilson for design/API discussion based on your feedback on #54968.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Add dotnetup project-local SDK setup spec" (#54971)

Issues.Title: Disable in-process parallel test execution
Issues.Body:
# Proposal: Disable In-Process Parallel Test Execution ## Summary This proposal recommends disabling in-process parallel test execution in `dotnet/sdk`. The current approach of running tests concurrently within a single process is causing persistent CI instability that imposes a significant tax on the team's productivity. ## Intermittent Test Failures Are Hindering PR Velocity Flaky tests caused by in-process parallelism are the single biggest source of friction in the PR merge workflow today. ### Obscuring signal and creating toil When a PR shows red CI, you cannot tell at a glance whether the failure is something you introduced or a pre-existing intermittent flake. To find out, you have to dig into every failure, examine the test, check if it's a known issue, and compare against other recent runs. This investigation takes time, creates frustration, and is a constant tax on every PR author and reviewer. The `/ba-g` (build analysis gate bypass) mechanism exists specifically to work around this, but its widespread use is a symptom of the problem, not a solution. ### Masking real regressions Because of the volume of noise, real regressions occasionally slip through unnoticed. When every CI run has failures, it becomes statistically inevitable that some genuine issues are overlooked. Not because people are negligent, but because the signal is drowned out by the noise. ### Wasting resources through retries When tests fail due to flakiness rather than a real problem, contributors retry CI, sometimes many times, hoping for a green run. This burns compute resources that are already in short supply and delays merges. ### The data Of the **100 most recently merged PRs** into `main` (2026-06-12 to 2026-06-23): | Classification | Percentage | |:---------------|:-----------| | Truly clean (no failures at all) | 37% | | Known build errors auto-handled | 18% | | Used `/ba-g` to bypass failures | 36% | | Green only after retries | 5% | | Failed CI, no `/ba-g` | 1% | | Other (neutral) | 3% | **Key takeaways:** - **Only 37%** of PRs merge with a genuinely clean CI run - **63% encounter test failures** that are either bypassed, auto-dismissed, or retried away > See [Appendix: PR Data](#appendix-pr-data) for the full list of 100 PRs with links, CI results, and classification. ## CI on `main` Is Consistently Red The `dotnet-sdk-public-ci` pipeline on `main` has a **3.7% pass rate** over the last ~30 days (2026-05-25 to 2026-06-23): only 4 out of 108 builds succeeded. The last green build was on **June 1, 2026**. A consistently red `main` branch means: - **No reliable baseline** to compare PR results against - **Regressions go undetected** because there's no "known good" state ## Known Build Errors Are Underutilized There are currently [23 open Known Build Error issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22Known+Build+Error%22) and [88 total filed historically](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue+label%3A%22Known+Build+Error%22). However, the 35% `/ba-g` rate above shows that the true scope of intermittent failures far exceeds what is documented. The Known Build Error system is underutilized because: - Defining a correct regex pattern requires careful effort and there is no tooling to assist - Overly broad patterns risk matching unrelated failures, which is dangerous - It is simply easier to `/ba-g` and move on A recent focused effort to properly document Known Build Errors has resulted in **17 issues filed in June 2026 alone**, compared to 0-2 per month for most of the prior year. This uptick is the result of actively investigating and cataloging the problem, not a sudden increase in flakiness. ## Root Cause: Writing Concurrent Test Code Is Difficult Writing test infrastructure that correctly supports concurrency is inherently difficult. The issues are endless and often difficult to diagnose. The test failures are not product code bugs. The problem is that there are no guardrails that promote writing concurrency-safe test code. It is easy for test authors to follow existing patterns that work in isolation but fail under parallel execution, and these issues are rarely surfaced locally. Retrofitting concurrency safety across the test codebase is an unbounded effort. ### Common patterns | Pattern | Description | |---------|-------------| | Shared file contention | Multiple tests write to the same temp files, binlogs, or solution files | | Resource cleanup races | One test cleans up shared resources while another is still using them | | Thread pool starvation | Leaked threads from watchers/pollers exhaust the thread pool | | Timing-dependent assertions | Tests rely on filesystem notifications or async coordination that breaks under load | | Directory traversal pollution | Tests discover files created by concurrent tests via parent directory walks | ### Illustrative example: [#54942](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54942) `PostActionTests.AddProjectToSolution_WithOutputRelativePath` fails intermittently because: 1. Test A creates a `.sln` file at the sandbox root directory using a temporary name (`GetTempName()`) 2. Test B, working in a subdirectory, discovers that `.sln` via solution discovery walking up the directory tree 3. Test A finishes and deletes its `.sln` 4. Test B tries to operate on the now-deleted `.sln` . **Failure** Key observations: - **The failing test is doing nothing wrong.** It's a different concurrent test that creates the conflict. - **Diagnosis is extremely difficult.** Resources use `GetTempName()` rather than being named in context of the owning test. There is no way to trace an artifact back to the test that created it. You cannot identify the offending test without first refactoring how artifacts are named across the codebase. - **These are not rare edge cases.** This pattern repeats across the entire test suite with variations on the theme. ### Additional examples | Issue | Problem | |-------|---------| | [#54942](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54942) | Solution file at sandbox root found by concurrent test via directory traversal, then deleted | | [#54817](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54817) | `IOException` from concurrent access to `msbuild.binlog` file | | [#54176](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54176) | Leaked `PollingDirectoryWatcher` threads cause thread pool starvation and hangs | | [#53661](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/53661) | Hot-reload file change detection races assertion timeout under concurrent load | | [#53870](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/53870) | Locked-file warning emission is timing-sensitive; sometimes sees 0 warnings | | [#44878](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/44878) | VS instance discovery has unintended concurrent access to a critical section | | [#9673](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/9673) | Restore tests had to serialize because parallel network operations caused flakiness | ### Why these are not fixable at scale - **Shared global/process resources:** Files, caches, watchers, thread pool, VS discovery, all shared by default. - **Async timing depends on machine load:** Filesystem events, thread scheduling, and network operations behave differently under contention. These failures rarely reproduce locally, which is how the problem grew undetected for so long. - **Tests assume isolation:** Many tests were written assuming sequential execution; they share implicit state without knowing it. - **Diagnosis requires refactoring:** You cannot identify the offending test without first making all test artifacts traceable, which is itself a large-scale refactoring effort. - **No return on investment:** Time spent making test infrastructure concurrency-safe is time not spent improving the product or adding meaningful test coverage. ## Precedent: Roslyn's Approach The Roslyn team (`dotnet/roslyn`) faced the exact same problem and made the deliberate decision to **disable in-process parallelism** years ago. Their approach is well-established and battle-tested. ### What Roslyn does 1. **Disabled xUnit parallelization**: their `eng/config/xunit.runner.json` sets `"parallelizeTestCollections": false` 2. **Multi-process local execution**: their `RunTests.dll` tool launches multiple subprocesses (`1.5 x processor count`), each running a test assembly in isolation 3. **Helix distribution for CI**: test assemblies are partitioned by historical execution time and distributed across multiple Helix machines ### Why Roslyn disabled parallelism From [roslyn#80095](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/issues/80095): > "Naive single-process parallelization wasn't tractable; workspace APIs and native components were problematic." From [roslyn#321](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/issues/321): > "xUnit 2.0 will run tests in parallel and several of our tests fail in that mode. Disabling for now." The pattern is identical to what `dotnet/sdk` is experiencing today. ### Roslyn's results By separating parallelism from isolation (run many processes, each sequential), Roslyn achieves: - **Fast CI** through Helix distribution across machines - **Fast local runs** through multi-process execution - **Reliable tests** because each process has full isolation - **No whack-a-mole**: no need to chase concurrency bugs in test infrastructure ## Recommendation ### Immediate action: Disable in-process parallelism 1. Set `parallelizeTestCollections: false` in xUnit runner configuration 2. Set `MSTestParallelizeScope: None` for MSTest projects 3. Validate CI stability improves This disables running multiple tests concurrently within a single process. Tests that are themselves written to exercise concurrency in the product (e.g., testing parallel workloads) are unaffected, as their internal concurrency is not controlled by the test runner. ### Expected outcomes - **Dramatic reduction in intermittent test failures** (expected 90%+) as cross-test contamination is eliminated by isolation. A small number of tests with internal concurrency issues (e.g., [#54960](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54960)) will remain, but the vast majority of current flakiness is caused by inter-test parallelism. - **CI pass rate recovers** from 3.7% toward a healthy baseline - **`/ba-g` usage drops** as flaky tests stabilize - **Known Build Error backlog shrinks** as root causes are eliminated by isolation - **Developer productivity improves**: less time retrying CI, investigating flakes, filing issues ### Cost The primary concern is **wall-clock execution time**. Disabling in-process parallelism without compensating will make individual test runs slower. However: - Helix already provides machine-level distribution for CI - The current "fast but broken" parallel execution provides **no value** if tests are unreliable; 63% of PRs encounter intermittent test failures, so the speed is illusory **Measured Helix compute impact (PR [#54943](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54943)):** *With parallelism disabled:* | Build | Helix Work Items | Total Compute (machine-hours) | |:------|:-----------------|:------------------------------| | [First run](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1477650) | 693 | 35.85 | | [Second run](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1478060) | 693 | 36.83 | | [Third run](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1479308) | 693 | 37.88 | | **Average** | **693** | **36.85** | *Baseline (parallelism enabled, 5 recent green PRs):* | PR | Helix Work Items | Total Compute (machine-hours) | |:---|:-----------------|:------------------------------| | [#54859](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54859) | 688 | 30.74 | | [#54792](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54792) | 691 | 31.63 | | [#54704](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54704) | 691 | 32.07 | | [#54748](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54748) | 688 | 33.77 | | [#54896](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54896) | 691 | 33.65 | | **Average** | **690** | **32.37** | Average shows a **+4.5 hour (~14%) increase** in total Helix agent compute. This is modest given the stability benefits. ### Long-term: Decide whether to reinvest in concurrent test execution Disabling parallelism solves the immediate crisis. The longer-term question is whether there is sufficient ROI in making the test suite concurrency-safe. This would be a sustained, ongoing investment, not a one-time fix. If we decide the ROI is there, the recommended approach is to re-enable parallelism **on a per-assembly basis**. Some test assemblies are likely better authored for concurrency than others. Rather than an all-or-nothing switch, we would evaluate each assembly individually and opt in only those that demonstrate stability under concurrent execution. This incremental approach limits risk, lets us measure the benefit of each addition, and could recover some or all of the ~14% Helix compute regression by re-enabling parallelism for the assemblies where it works reliably. For other assemblies where concurrency issues are more deeply embedded, the ROI of making them thread-safe may not justify the investment. If we decide the ROI is not there, multi-process execution (as Roslyn does) provides an alternative path to fast local test runs without requiring concurrency-safe test code. Each process runs a test assembly in isolation, and parallelism comes from running many processes simultaneously. This would only apply to the local development experience; in CI, Helix already provides machine-level distribution across work items, so multi-process execution is not needed there. Regardless of the parallelism decision, there are follow-up investments we can make to improve test infrastructure robustness: - **Replace raw `GetTempPath` usage** with a test-aware alternative that provides isolation and traceability - **Write analyzers to catch bad patterns** (e.g., shared mutable state, non-unique temp paths, unsynchronized file access) before they reach CI - These improvements reduce future risk whether or not parallelism is eventually re-enabled ## Conclusion Over 63% of PRs encounter intermittent test failures, and more than a third require `/ba-g` to merge. This is not a sustainable state. The underlying concurrency issues are systemic, difficult to diagnose, and intractable to fix individually. Continuing down the path of addressing these failures one by one is not enough. This problem is affecting us right now, and we need to get it under control. Disabling in-process parallelism eliminates the root cause immediately. Once stability is restored, we can then decide whether the long-term ROI of making the test suite concurrency-safe justifies the sustained investment required, or whether alternative approaches to fast execution better serve the team. ---

Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Disable in-process parallel test execution" (#54967)

Issues.Title: Port first-run experience to the NativeAOT CLI entrypoint
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Continues the AOT `dotnet` CLI arc by bringing the cross-cutting **first-run experience** — previously implemented only in the managed `Program.cs` — to the NativeAOT entrypoint, and aligns the two entrypoints structurally so parity is easy to verify.

This is being opened to exercise the changes in CI (notably the AOT publish/test legs across platforms).

## What's here

- **Shared first-run logic.** Extract the first-run experience into `src/Cli/dotnet/FirstRunExperience.cs`, used by both the managed CLI and the AOT bridge (`NativeEntryPoint`), instead of living inline in `Program.cs`.
- **Shared command invocation.** Extract the in-process command invocation into `CommandInvocation.ExecuteInternalCommand` so `NativeEntryPoint` no longer references `Program`. Both entrypoints call the same method.
- **In-process workload detection.** `WorkloadInstallDetector` detects installed workloads in-process by **reusing** the existing `RegistryWorkloadInstallationRecordRepository` read path. The elevation/MSI-IPC write members and `InstallerBase` are `#if`'d away for AOT rather than reimplemented. The AOT first run now only defers to the managed CLI when workload **repair** is actually pending.
- **In-process dev-cert generation on AOT.** Generate the ASP.NET Core HTTPS developer certificate in-process under NativeAOT. The `CertificateManager` uses only BCL X509/RSA crypto (no reflection/dynamic code); on macOS the crypto PAL resolves dynamically against the host's already-loaded native library, so the existing `RemoveAppleCryptoFromNativeAotLink` workaround is preserved.
- **No `Program.cs` in dotnet-aot.** `dotnet-aot` is a `NativeLib=Shared` library whose entry points are the `[UnmanagedCallersOnly]` exports in `NativeEntryPoint` — it never invoked a managed `Program.Main`, so the linked managed `Program.cs` is dropped entirely (not replaced with a project-local one). The managed `dotnet.dll` keeps its own `Program.cs` (used by the hostfxr fallback path), with the now-dead `#if CLI_AOT` branches removed. `DESIGN.md` updated to match.

## Validation (local, Windows)

- AOT, managed CLI, and AOT test builds: **0 warnings / 0 errors**.
- AOT publish (full ILC native codegen, with no `Program.cs`): **0 warnings / 0 errors** — no IL2026/IL3050.
- AOT test suite: **77 passed / 6 skipped / 0 failed** (the 6 skip because the AOT `dn` binary isn't built in Debug).

## AOT size impact

Measured by publishing `dotnet-aot` (Release, `win-x64`, full ILC) on this branch vs. the PR base commit (`793ebf24`), then diffing the `dotnet-aot.mstat` files with `sizoscope-cli` (same methodology as `.github/workflows/aot-size-analysis.yml`).

| Native `dotnet-aot.dll` | Size |
| --- | --- |
| Base (`793ebf24`) | 16.846 MB (17,664,000 B) |
| This PR | 17.438 MB (18,285,056 B) |
| **Delta** | **+606 KB (+3.52%)** |

`sizoscope-cli` accounted difference: **+490.7 kB**, attributable entirely to the first-run features ported in-process:

| Contributor | Δ | Reason |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `System.Security.Cryptography` | +133.1 kB | dev-cert generation |
| `System.Private.CoreLib` | +120 kB | crypto/registry surface |
| `System.Security.AccessControl` | +39.0 kB | registry ACL read |
| `System.Linq` | +18.5 kB | detection/cert code |
| `Microsoft.AspNetCore.DeveloperCertificates.XPlat` | +15.8 kB | cert generator package |
| `System.Formats.Asn1` | +9.5 kB | cert encoding |
| `System.Security.Principal.Windows` | +8.6 kB | registry identity |
| `Microsoft.Deployment.DotNet.Releases` | +6.7 kB | workload band detection |
| `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WorkloadManifestReader` | +5.6 kB | workload detection |
| `NuGet.Common` / `Cli.Utils` / `Configurer` / `Win32.Registry` | ~11 kB | workload record repo reuse |

The large `*.resources` rows in the raw `sizoscope` output appear with equal `+`/`−` magnitudes (localized satellites re-attributed across builds) and net to ~zero — which is why the accounted total (490.7 kB) is below the raw file delta (606 kB). No unexpected dependencies were pulled in.

## Notes for reviewers

- The macOS runtime cert path (`/usr/bin/security import` + dynamic crypto PAL resolution with the static archive removed) compiles AOT-clean but could not be runtime-validated locally on Windows — **this PR is partly here to validate that on macOS CI.**

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Issues.Title: Add dotnetup local SDK setup
Issues.Body:
## Summary
- Adds `dotnetup sdk install --local` / root install `--local` for project-local SDK setup.
- Installs into the nearest project `.dotnet` directory and configures `global.json` with `sdk.paths: [".dotnet", "$host$"]`.
- Preserves existing `global.json` sections, supports exact/channel/prerelease settings, updates `.gitignore` idempotently, and avoids PATH/profile/default install mutations.
- Adds unit, parser, and end-to-end coverage plus docs for the new local install flow.

## Validation
- Built `src\Installer\dotnetup\dotnetup.csproj` with `/p:_DotnetupSkipPublishOnBuild=true`.
- Built `test\dotnetup.Tests\dotnetup.Tests.csproj` with isolated `ArtifactsDir`.
- Ran focused tests: `GlobalJsonUpdateTests`, `ParserTests`, `LibraryTests`, local install E2E, env script E2E, and stdin forwarding E2E.
- Ran manual throwaway-project E2E for exact local install, existing `global.json` preservation, nested global.json discovery, invalid/missing version errors, `.gitignore` newline/idempotence, and preview local install.
- Ran full `dotnetup.Tests`; local result has one pre-existing environment-specific failure in `WindowsPathHelperTests.GetProgramFilesDotnetPaths_ReturnsValidPaths` because this machine returns `C:\Program Files\dotnet\x64`.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Add dotnetup local SDK setup" (#54968)

Issues.Title: Ignore MTP exit code 8 (zero tests ran) for Helix work items
Issues.Body:
## Problem

Some Helix work items fail with **exit code 8** even though the test results tab shows everything green, which is confusing and has led to PRs being merged in a falsely-failing state (see #54963, #54788).

Exit code `8` in Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP) means [**"the test session ran zero tests"**](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/testing/microsoft-testing-platform-troubleshooting#exit-codes).

## Root cause

The affected assemblies (`dotnet-aot.Tests`, `Microsoft.Win32.Msi.Tests`, …) are all `MSTest.Sdk` → MTP projects. They run through the `isMTPProject` branch in `SDKCustomCreateXUnitWorkItemsWithTestExclusion`, invoked as `dotnet exec <dll> --filter ...`.

A work item ends up running zero tests when:
- class-based sharding (`--filter`) isolates a partition containing only skipped tests, or
- a project legitimately runs no tests on a platform (e.g. Windows-only Msi tests on Linux/macOS).

MTP then returns exit code 8 and Helix treats the nonzero exit as a failure.

## Fix

Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` to the MTP work-item command so "zero tests ran" reports success instead of failing the work item. This is the mitigation called out in the issue.

- Scoped to the MTP branch only — the VSTest `dotnet test` path doesn't use MTP exit codes and is untouched.
- Real failures (exit 2, crashes, setup errors, etc.) still surface.

The stricter long-term fix (relaxing exit 8 on the test-platform side when tests exist but are all skipped) is tracked separately.

Fixes #54963
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Ignore MTP exit code 8 (zero tests ran) for Helix work items" (#54965)

Issues.Title: [automated] Merge branch 'main' => 'release/dnup'
Issues.Body:
I detected changes in the main branch which have not been merged yet to release/dnup. I'm a robot and am configured to help you automatically keep release/dnup up to date, so I've opened this PR. This PR merges commits made on main by the following committers: * Evangelink ## Instructions for merging from UI This PR will not be auto-merged. When pull request checks pass, complete this PR by creating a merge commit, *not* a squash or rebase commit. If this repo does not allow creating merge commits from the GitHub UI, use command line instructions. ## Instructions for merging via command line Run these commands to merge this pull request from the command line. ``` sh git fetch git checkout main git pull --ff-only git checkout release/dnup git pull --ff-only git merge --no-ff main # If there are merge conflicts, resolve them and then run git merge --continue to complete the merge # Pushing the changes to the PR branch will re-trigger PR validation. git push https://github.com/dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
or if you are using SSH ``` git push git@github.com:dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
After PR checks are complete push the branch ``` git push ``` ## Instructions for resolving conflicts :warning: If there are merge conflicts, you will need to resolve them manually before merging. You can do this [using GitHub][resolve-github] or using the [command line][resolve-cli]. [resolve-github]: https://help.github.com/articles/resolving-a-merge-conflict-on-github/ [resolve-cli]: https://help.github.com/articles/resolving-a-merge-conflict-using-the-command-line/ ## Instructions for updating this pull request Contributors to this repo have permission update this pull request by pushing to the branch 'merge/main-to-release/dnup'. This can be done to resolve conflicts or make other changes to this pull request before it is merged. The provided examples assume that the remote is named 'origin'. If you have a different remote name, please replace 'origin' with the name of your remote. ``` git fetch git checkout -b merge/main-to-release/dnup origin/release/dnup git pull https://github.com/dotnet/sdk merge/main-to-release/dnup (make changes) git commit -m "Updated PR with my changes" git push https://github.com/dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
or if you are using SSH ``` git fetch git checkout -b merge/main-to-release/dnup origin/release/dnup git pull git@github.com:dotnet/sdk merge/main-to-release/dnup (make changes) git commit -m "Updated PR with my changes" git push git@github.com:dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
Contact .NET Core Engineering (dotnet/dnceng) if you have questions or issues. Also, if this PR was generated incorrectly, help us fix it. See https://github.com/dotnet/arcade/blob/main/.github/workflows/scripts/inter-branch-merge.ps1.

Issues.Title: .NET 11 Preview 5
Issues.Body:
> This issue is a locked mirror of https://github.com/dotnet/core/discussions/10445. See that thread for discussion.

We are pleased to announce the release of .NET 11 Preview 5. Follow the main announcement and [blog announcement](https://aka.ms/dotnet/11/preview5).

Browse the release notes for a full list of improvements in this release:

- [.NET MAUI](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/dotnetmaui.md)
- [ASP.NET Core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/aspnetcore.md)
- [C#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/csharp.md)
- [F#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/fsharp.md)
- [Container images](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/containers.md)
- [EF Core & Data](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/efcore.md)
- [Libraries](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/libraries.md)
- [MSBuild](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/msbuild.md)
- [NuGet](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/nuget.md)
- [Runtime](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/runtime.md)
- [SDK](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/sdk.md)
- [Windows Forms](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/winforms.md)
- [WPF](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/wpf.md)

## Get started

To get started with .NET 11, [install the .NET 11 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/11.0).

If you're on Windows using Visual Studio, we recommend installing the latest [Visual Studio 2026 Insiders](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/insiders/). You can also use Visual Studio Code and the [C# Dev Kit](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-dotnettools.csdevkit) extension with .NET 11.

Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in ASP.NET Core SignalR and Blazor Server. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in the MessagePack hub protocol used by SignalR and Blazor Server where an attacker can send deeply-nested MessagePack arrays to cause a stack overflow, resulting in a denial of service. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/67100 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 10.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 8.0. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.28 Runtime or .NET 8.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.17 Runtime or .NET 9.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.9 Runtime or .NET 10.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45591]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45591) ### Acknowledgements Anonymous ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45491 | .NET Tampering Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in System.Formats.Tar. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A tampering vulnerability exists in the `TarFile.ExtractToDirectory` method where a symlink path traversal enables an attacker to perform arbitrary file writes outside the intended extraction directory. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/129184 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** Medium - **Score:** 6.8 - version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 10.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 8.0. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.28 Runtime or .NET 8.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.17 Runtime or .NET 9.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.9 Runtime or .NET 10.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45491]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45491) ### Acknowledgements Anonymous, Ashmit Sharma, Ky0toFu, phrolo7a ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45490 | .NET SDK Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their environments to remove this vulnerability. An elevation of privilege vulnerability exists in the .NET SDK `dotnet.exe` workload command where one local user can exploit a weakly ACLed named pipe to perform arbitrary file creation or truncation as another local user. This only affects .NET on Windows operating systems. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54652 ## Mitigation factors Microsoft has not identified any mitigating factors for this vulnerability. ## Affected software * .NET 10.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 10.0.100, <= 10.0.108|10.0.109| |>= 10.0.300, <= 10.0.300|10.0.301| * .NET 9.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 9.0.100, <= 9.0.117|9.0.118| |>= 9.0.300, <= 9.0.314|9.0.315| * .NET 8.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 8.0.100, <= 8.0.127|8.0.128| |>= 8.0.400, <= 8.0.421|8.0.422| ## Affected Packages The vulnerability does not affect Microsoft .NET packages. ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If you have an SDK with a version listed, you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0 SDK, .NET 9.0 SDK, or .NET 10.0 SDK, as appropriate. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 8.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 9.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 10.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. * You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. You will see output like the following; ``` .NET SDK: Version: 10.0.100 Commit: 59db016f11 Runtime Environment: OS Name: Windows OS Version: 10.0.26100 OS Platform: Windows RID: win-x64 Base Path: C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\10.0.100\ .NET SDKs installed: 10.0.100 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk] .NET runtimes installed: Microsoft.AspNetCore.App 10.0.0 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.AspNetCore.App] Microsoft.NETCore.App 10.0.0 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App] ``` Once you have installed the updated SDK, close any open command prompts, terminals, IDEs, or build agents that may have loaded an older `dotnet.exe`, and then re-run the affected `dotnet workload` command using the updated SDK. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, please email details to [secure@microsoft.com](mailto:secure@microsoft.com). Reports may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Core & .NET 5 Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/sdk. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45490](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45490) ### Acknowledgements Ky0toFu ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: .NET 11 Preview 4
Issues.Body:
> This issue is a locked mirror of https://github.com/dotnet/core/discussions/10415. See that thread for discussion.

We are pleased to announce the release of .NET 11 Preview 4. Follow the main announcement and [blog announcement](https://aka.ms/dotnet/11/preview4).

Browse the release notes for a full list of improvements in this release:

- [.NET MAUI](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/dotnetmaui.md)
- [ASP.NET Core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/aspnetcore.md)
- [C#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/csharp.md)
- [F#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/fsharp.md)
- [Container images](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/containers.md)
- [EF Core & Data](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/efcore.md)
- [Libraries](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/libraries.md)
- [MSBuild](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/msbuild.md)
- [NuGet](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/nuget.md)
- [Runtime](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/runtime.md)
- [SDK](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/sdk.md)
- [Windows Forms](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/winforms.md)
- [WPF](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/wpf.md)

## Get started

To get started with .NET 11, [install the .NET 11 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/11.0).

If you're on Windows using Visual Studio, we recommend installing the latest [Visual Studio 2026 Insiders](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/insiders/). You can also use Visual Studio Code and the [C# Dev Kit](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-dotnettools.csdevkit) extension with .NET 11.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-32177 – .NET Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. Heap-based buffer overflow in .NET allows an unauthorized attacker to elevate privileges locally. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/wpf/issues/11632 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.3 - **Vector:** AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L/E:U/RL:O/RC:C - **Weakness:** CWE-122: Heap-based Buffer Overflow, CWE-20: Improper Input Validation ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** Windows - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 ### .NET 9 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 ### .NET 8 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0, NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, as appropriate. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-32177]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-32177) ### Acknowledgements Ky0toFu ### Revisions V1.0 (May 12, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-42899 – ASP.NET Core Denial of Service Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. Loop with unreachable exit condition ('infinite loop') in ASP.NET Core allows an unauthorized attacker to deny service over a network. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/66659 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H/E:U/RL:O/RC:C - **Weakness:** CWE-835: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop') ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- |
